sentences.text: With the onset of the anti-Jewish laws and bylaws, the neighbors and the population around us simply started to be afraid.
They knew perfectly well that they are not supposed to have any contact with Jews.
Any kind of help, any kind of association would mean arrest and further, probably transportation to a camp.

Now, one of the German-- one of the special orders was to put more Jews together somehow to concentrate them, so that some people were moved out of their homes and moved to another family's, to-- to another Jewish family.
Like, to our apartment came [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] another six persons.
They were three families.
Two of them we knew very well.
And then was one couple who were originally from Germany and one couple who was, I think, from Austria caught in the territory of Czechoslovakia.
In others words, they didn't leave in time.
Now, the same happened practically to every Jewish family, so that they have been condensed together.
Now, this everybody in Prague was registered with the police, which still exists simply that your domicile is registered with the police.
In other words, if you are looking for somebody you can go just to the police registry, and you found out where the person lives.
At that point it was extremely tight.
We had a-- a kind of a ID, passes.
And on the ID pass of every Jew was written J as Jew, so that it was quite obvious if you would have to show a document so everybody would know.
I can tell you only one family name.
And one of them, it was a Reiter, R-E-I-T-E-R.
His first name was Karel Charles, K-A-R-E-L.
And her name was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sonja, S-O-N-J-A.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were-- they lived in a neighboring town of my grandfather's.
So I knew him as a little boy.
And the guy also was in camp in Auschwitz, and then in the Schwarzheide with me.
I'll spell the Schwarzheide later for you.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think they both survived.
The other families, I forgot their names.
And they didn't make it.
They were transported with the first transport which were leaving Prague.
Now, if you want I will come to the transports.
That was very simple.
You just put their cots.
And you put their-- I mean, kind of from the couches you put the pillows on the ground, and you managed.
We used the kitchen as a communal kitchen.
And in our household, there was really no friction.
I don't remember any friction.
In some households, it caused, naturally, this limitation I think.
People-- people didn't catch up so quickly what is happening.
So we caught up rather quickly.
You know, when you are being restricted and when your kind of privacy is limited by several dozens of laws and bylaws, you know that something is-- something far more odious is striking.
So you value your privacy.
But you value the fact that you've got a room for yourself.
There was a-- the Germans operated in a very peculiar way.
Since 1933-- in 1933 already, they created a special section which had something to do with the solution of the Jewish Problem.
It became a part of the SS.

And that, as such, simply had the task to make, eventually, the territories which were occupied by Germans or which became part of Germany-- like, Czechoslovakia was split into Bohemia and Moravia, which became a protectorate of Boohmen und Mahren.
B- O, there's two O, H-M-E-N.
And capital M-A, those two things on it, H-R-E-N with a special kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ruler over the-- over this territory called the protectorate.
He was the protector.
His name was Heydrich, H-E-Y-D-R-I-C-H, who was known as 'the butcher.' He was, in the Nazi hierarchy, about number three after Hitler.
He was considered to be become eventually the Hitler successor, for he was relatively young.
Now, the Department for the Solution of the Jewish Question moved to Prague with the army and with the SS.
And the-- they simply were preparing to make the territory free of Jews.
Judenrein, J-U-D-E-N-R-E-I-N.
That was the goal of any part of Germany or the occupied territories.

They do it-- did it in a way that they themselves ordered the Jewish community or, as it was called at that point in time, the Council of the Elders to prepare for eventual transportation of the Jews out of Prague or out of the territory, out of the territory of the protectorate.
And it started in 1940 when a few transports were sent to Upper Silezia, S-I-L-E-Z-I-A, which was part of Poland at that point.
And then they sent a huge transport from Prague, 5,000 people, to Lodz.
It's in Poland.
It's this L-- cut through and pronounced in a soft way, 'lah--' O-D-Z.
The Germans called it Litzmanstadt, L-I-T-Z-M-A-N-S-T-A-D-T.
And from then on, every week or every two weeks usually 1,000 or 2,000 people were sent to first to different places in Poland, in 1940, 1941, 1942.
In 1941, they started to send people to a-- a fortress, which was built under the reign of Empress Maria Theresa.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You'll spell.

[LAUGHS] And it was called Theresienstadt, T-H-E-R-I-E-N-S-T-A-D-T or Terezin into Czech, T-E-R-E-Z-I-N, which was a fortress, military fortress, with huge walls and a moat around it and two gates, main gates into it.
And the Czechs, they kept it pretty well-preserved.
And there was a small town in the middle of the fortress, inhabitants about 7,000.
And there were about four huge military barracks.
There was the military, Czech military was located there, I think originally whether it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just plain air forces.
I don't know exactly what kind of part of military.
But altogether, there were about 7,000 soldiers there.
And the Germans conceived the idea that they would evacuate this thing which-- and they will put Czech gendarmes as guards around it.
And it will be very easy, simply, to put there a large quantity of Jews.
And it was very, relatively easy to guard it.
So they started to send, first, people who are preparing this kind of move.
They evacuated the Czechs.
There was no  military, naturally, there.
And the-- that place which had space for about 11,000 people and soldiers in the peak of its production had about 77,000 Jews in it, pretty overcrowded.
And that became kind of a sifting station.
From there, they were sending then Jews, again, to the east, to Poland.
We, somehow, when the transportation started-- I'm skipping many things-- when the transportation started we knew that something very, very-- I will repeat the name-- odious is happening.
For there was naturally a blackout on the news.
But we received occasionally a postcard from a camp, like from Sehelmn, S-E-H-E-L-M-N on which was written-- I'm translating, I'm well off.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am healthy.
And I'm-- we came here on this and this date.
And I am together with brother Hans.
And I am expected-- I'm expecting that within in the next couple of days I'll be also with Uncle Otto.
Please send parcels to the address below.
And there was some address.
We looked at it and says, Uncle Otto died 10 years ago.
What's happening?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hans is going probably bonkers.
Must be, I mean, so bad if they don't have enough to eat.
But the translation was quite clear cut.
And I was young.
And I realized this means only one thing.
This means death.
It means the destruction.
So I started to look at that point for something that I would be protected from being put into the transport.
And I got myself into a training as a male nurse.
They created there a small hospital for Jews.
They are bringing Jews from all part of Bohemia and Moravia, mostly old people.
Old people-- most were 20 years younger than I am now.
But when you are 16 or 17 or 18, everybody's old.
And we got six weeks training, and I was a nurse.
I knew how to give injections, intravenous injections, how to cut and dress people, how to do all kinds of changing this and similar, which helped me when I became finally a physician.
And I realized when I put down ss enema, soap suds enema, that it means something else, that it doesn't mean only the order, that somebody has to do it.
But as such I was protected.
I had also a pass by which I could take a street car, which was permitted only for certain Jews on the back platform of the-- of the street car.
And only one could use it only till 8 o'clock in the evening when there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Curfew, thank you, the curfew.
But my pass simply went unlimited.
So it happened that, I don't know, I was stopped by a German saying, a Jew, what are you doing here?
And the came somebody who was obviously from the German secret police, the toughest of the toughest.
He says, let the damn Jew be.
He's got already the permit to do it.
So much courage, any chutzpah he doesn't have.
And as such, simply, I-- at that point, I already joined the Zionist movement.
And we were trained.
We were prepared to go down to Vienna, like the transport previously.
There we would be put on a ship.
Usually, they took 1,000 young people and 500 old people.
And you had to give up everything.
And it cost, I don't know, so and so much, so many thousands marks to be permitted to leave the territory.
These were boats which were boat by-- bought by the Jewish Congress, usually Romanian-- old Romanian coal boats.
And when they have been full so, they let them go down the River Danube.
They rest, probably you know.
The Hungarians closed their eyes.
The Romanians closed the eyes.
They appeared in the Black Sea.
The Russians, in spite of what was happening, closed their eyes and let the boat pass.
The Turks, for a certain amount of money, let them pass through Bosphorus and Dardanelles and they appeared in the Aegean Sea.
The-- the Greeks didn't mind.
And then they'd be turning towards around Turkey to-- between the Cyprus and the mainland.
There they were being picked up by the British flotilla, which would follow them up to the coast.
The rest you know.
I didn't get so far.
Oh yes, yes, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One of them was my friend whose name was the Reichmann, R-E-I-C-H-M-A-N-N.
The first name was Richard.
I could give you some names, but they wouldn't probably mean too much.
Yeah.
I really do.
We were even permitted to be trained, you know, by the SS, permitted us to be trained in British [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] army commands.
Like, we had to know, we have to-- we had to be aware that you have to probably jump overboard, swim to the sea-- seashore, that there were people from the kibbutz.
And we'll mix into it.
And then we come to the coast, so we will be arrested by the British.
And we will be interrogated now.
We had to know Hebrew.
We had to know some kind of English.
And we had to know the British commands.

And they let us have some kind of wooden sticks by which we trained on all kinds of the [INAUDIBLE] and similar, the Hebrew commands or the British commands which were quite different from the commands in the Czech army or the Czech boy scouts or-- or the Y-camp or whatever-- where ever I came before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] return to the Department of the Solution of the Jewish Question.
Interestingly enough, they knew plenty of the Zionist leaders, these SS Men.
For the Zionists, they have the same goal as the Germans wanted them to have.
And that is to get the Jews out of Europe.
In the '30s, simply it was done simply by release, by paying certain amount of money and, later on, simply by seizure of all the property and everything.

But, interestingly enough, when it came to the-- some kind of a leadership in the ghettos or the leadership in the camps, so many times these were some Zionists who were known to the SS who just picked them up at random for somebody new.
That they are capable at least to talk to them, and they knew what it is all about.
And why I am saying this simply, it  is good to point for at one certain moment in time, Eva and her mother, her father, was arrested at that point, was in Czech prison, was put in a transport.
And I tried somehow to get her out.
Meaning, I went to the Council of the Elders where I knew one of the Zionists.
And it is where this is explained to me, this is totally impossible.
For we didn't put the transport, the names together.
It was done by the SS themselves.

It is still one of the penalty transports for the death of the protector Heydrich, who was shot, who was killed by a Czech, by a Czech parachutist who had parachuted from Britain and succeeded to kill him.
The retribution was something absolutely enormous.
About 40,000 people were killed.
And many transports with Jew were sent towards the east.
And this was supposed to be one of the transports.
And I said, but something, maybe something could be done.
He says, the only thing is that we might get in touch with people in Theresienstadt, in Terezin, where the transport will stay for about a day.
It will be shuttled then directly towards Poland.
Maybe we'll succeed to get you out, you and Eva.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is absolutely no guarantee.
If you want to, try it, try it.
How do I go around it?
He says, you have to get the permission from the Gestapo, G-E-A-S-T-A-P-O, the Geheime Staatspolizei, meaning the secret-- the secret German police, the dreaded secret German police.
And with their permission, you can join the transport.
So here I was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was 19 years old.
I was in the Gestapo before, for I forgot my pass at home once.
And I reported, and I was let out.
And here I was to ask simply their permission.
The SS Men who was-- it was kind of an old fashioned building which belonged to a Jew, Baron Betschek, B-E-T-S-C-H-E-K, who was in the United States meanwhile.
And they made headquarters, Gestapo headquarters where I reported.
And I had no recommendation.
I simply just explained what I want.
And the SS Man who was in charge was sitting behind a desk let me stand.
And he said, your name?
He wrote it down.
He says, in German said to me, hmm.
When you are totally crazy, I'll let you go.
You report to the Division of the Solution of the Jewish Problem, which was in another part of the city.
You have to go straight there, for the transport will be leaving in the evening.
So I took a street car in there.
There was another type of SS Man who had something to do with the Jews and with the transport.
There I reported.
He just, I mean, took my name, put it down.
He put it, I don't know, to the type writer.
And then he asked two SS Men to bring me to my home to pick up my things and bring me directly to the gathering place.
And that was my first trip in a Mercedes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where I was sitting in the middle and two guarded-- two armed SS Men next to me were taking me to Prague 12.
And I picked up, explained to my parents, they shouldn't worry, that we'll manage somehow.
And my father gave me a Hebrew blessing, let me kneel down, gave the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I left.
They took me to the gathering place.
And we left that evening.
Left, meaning, we walked towards a train.
We boarded the train.
It was still, I mean, a personal train.
And we went to Terezin where we walked about 2 miles.
Later on, they had a special track going directly to Terezin about a year later.
And they walked us in guarded to a special gathering place which was part of old-- one of the old military barracks.
And we were simply closed off.
We were not permitted to move out.
We simply were waiting just for further transportation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- during the night, there came one Czech gendarme with two Jewish persons from the Council of the Elders in Theresienstadt.
And he had the permission to take out four persons.
One of them was somebody whose name I forgot.
One of them was Eva's mother.
One of them was Eva.
And one of them was I.
And we left.
And the transport left within 12 hours, went to Sobibor, S-O-B-I-B-O-R.
The people lived probably another 36 hours.
Now, that was how I got-- how we got to Theresienstadt.
Any question?
Or, am I off?
Dr.
Roden, do you remember the name of the Zionist representative on the Council of Elders in Prague who originally helped you get into transport?
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] His name was simple.
His name was Stein, the same as my mother's-- in my-- my-- my-- good slip-- my wife's family, S-T-E-I-N.
And the person who was responsible in Theresienstadt, his name was Edelstein, E-D-E-L-S-T-E-I-N.
He was a great man who simply shielded and saved anything and anybody who can, and who mostly shielded the young people.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And due him-- he did not survive-- but due to him and who followed then him as the Chief of the-- Chief of the Council of the Elders, some people, some young people survived even in Theresienstadt til the end of the war.
My parents came about not even a month later after us.
And Eva's mother was in the same transport and as Eva and a vignette.
[INAUDIBLE].
The reason how I justify that I want to join my bride for the SS--
OK.
